elements.sent-2411-004: Yu are with The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology, Clear Water Bay, Hong Kong {\tt \{czhaobb, lluoan, cjiangab, syuanaf\}@connect.ust.hk and {\tt \{cqf, hongyuyu\}@ust.hk .}}
elements.sent-2411-005: Robotic manipulation has made significant advancements, with systems demonstrating high precision and repeatability.
elements.sent-2411-006: However, this remarkable precision often fails to translate into efficient manipulation of thin deformable objects.
elements.sent-2411-007: Current robotic systems lack imprecise dexterity, the ability to perform dexterous manipulation through robust and adaptive behaviors that do not rely on precise control.
elements.sent-2411-008: This paper explores the singulation and grasping of thin, deformable objects.
elements.sent-2411-009: Here, we propose a novel solution that incorporates passive compliance, touch, and proprioception into thin, deformable object manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-010: Our system employs a soft, underactuated hand that provides passive compliance, facilitating adaptive and gentle interactions to dexterously manipulate deformable objects without requiring precise control.
elements.sent-2411-011: The tactile and force/torque sensors equipped on the hand, along with a depth camera, gather sensory data required for manipulation via the proposed slip module.
elements.sent-2411-012: The manipulation policies are learned directly from raw sensory data via model-free reinforcement learning, bypassing explicit environmental and object modeling.
elements.sent-2411-013: We implement a hierarchical double-loop learning process to enhance learning efficiency by decoupling the action space.
elements.sent-2411-014: Our method was deployed on real-world robots and trained in a self-supervised manner.
elements.sent-2411-015: The resulting policy was tested on a variety of challenging tasks that were beyond the capabilities of prior studies, ranging from displaying suit fabric like a salesperson to turning pages of sheet music for violinists.
elements.sent-2411-016: Thin, deformable objects are in many daily activities.
elements.sent-2411-017: For robots to be widely used in human living environments, they must be able to manipulate these objects.
elements.sent-2411-018: Despite notable progress in robotic manipulation \cite{fp1,fp2,fp3,fp4,fp5,fp6 and the deployment of numerous commercial robotic platforms \cite{fp7, fp8, fp9, fp10 with precision surpassing that of humans, even the most advanced robots fall short of human performance when manipulating thin, deformable objects.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: We suggest that advancements in thin deformable object manipulation hinge on achieving “imprecise dexterity” — the ability to perform dexterous manipulation through robust and adaptive behaviors despite imprecisions in control, movement, and perception.
elements.sent-2411-020: Singulation (isolating a single layer or item from a group or stack) and grasping thin, deformable objects are common daily tasks, such as turning book pages (Fig. \ref{fig:1 and Fig. \ref{fig:2 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-021: These tasks present intricate dynamics due to the thin structures, stacking tendencies, and high-dimensional configuration spaces of the objects, posing significant challenges in manipulation control and perception.
elements.sent-2411-022: Conventional robots, which rely on precise control, struggle to meet multiple objectives: adapting to surface deformation and slipping, maintaining stable contact, avoiding object damage, and ensuring successful singulation and grasping \cite{l6 .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Visual sensors often fail to capture essential physical properties like friction and stiffness and can be occluded by fingers or objects, necessitating heavy reliance on touch and proprioception for feedback on object interaction.
elements.sent-2411-024: While humans effortlessly perform such tasks with hands that integrate passive compliance, touch, and proprioception \cite{m4 , it remains an open challenge in robotics.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Previous work has not demonstrated robust systems for the singulation and grasping of thin, deformable objects, often being limited to a single object type \cite{l3, flipbot, revision_R2_1, revision_R2_2 or assuming an isolated object within the environment \cite{revision_R1_1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: In downstream applications such as robot-assisted dressing \cite{k1, k2 , garment folding \cite{k4 , and bagging \cite{k3 , researchers tend to overlook this critical step, often making assumptions about grasping or relying on heuristic strategies.}}}

elements.sent-2411-027: The key component is our soft, underactuated two-finger hand equipped with tactile and F/T sensors, offering passive compliance, touch, and muscle proprioception similar to a human hand.
elements.sent-2411-028: Different thin, deformable objects exhibit various behaviors under external forces; for instance, fabric denting easily while pages resist pressure but yield to shear forces.
elements.sent-2411-029: Our gripper's passive compliance allows its fingers to follow the object's deformation, maintaining continuous contact and limiting potentially damaging forces exerted on both objects and the robot \cite{l6 .}
elements.sent-2411-030: Such adaptability enables gentle, force-limited interactions, resulting in successful grasps without requiring perfect robot precision \cite{revision5, revision_R2_3 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: The underactuated design reduces control parameters and simplifies the manipulation, while the integrated sensors provide the foundation for measuring the environmental information necessary for the task.
elements.sent-2411-032: To drive our soft hand, we learned a direct mapping from perception to robot control using model-free RL in the real world.
elements.sent-2411-033: However, model-free RL alone was insufficient for learning a robust singulation and grasping policy.
elements.sent-2411-034: Therefore, we incorporated and validated several additional components to achieve the demonstrated capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-035: The first is a slip module.
elements.sent-2411-036: We found the reward sparsity in these tasks makes robot impossible to discover effective policies from scratch, and acquiring task-relevant perception remains a problem.
elements.sent-2411-037: To address these, we employ an active exploratory motion called slip, where robot fingers slide over the object's surface.
elements.sent-2411-038: Slip motion is guided by a neural network that receives inputs from a wrist-mounted camera and is trained on data reflecting human intuition (e.g., turning book pages from the upper right).
elements.sent-2411-039: Such position-specific slips provide the robot with a proper initial pose, reducing the need for exploration.
elements.sent-2411-040: Meanwhile, the deformation brought by the interaction reveals unobservable physical properties like flexural rigidity and friction, which are captured by the tactile and F/T sensors.
elements.sent-2411-041: Another component is a hierarchical, dual-loop learning structure.
elements.sent-2411-042: In essence, the dual-loop structure lies in the structured and efficient exploration and exploitation of the action space.
elements.sent-2411-043: The necessary action range after slip motion varies due to differences in material surface properties and movement errors caused by depth camera noise.
elements.sent-2411-044: The outer loop selects between coarse and fine action spaces based on a required range of movement, while the inner loop outputs concrete robot actions.
elements.sent-2411-045: By selecting the appropriate action space based on current perception and learned experience, unnecessary action exploration is reduced.
elements.sent-2411-046: The presented study achieves a significant step toward human-level sensorimotor intelligence.
elements.sent-2411-047: Our key contribution is an approach for singulation and grasping thin, deformable objects by controlling a soft, underactuated hand that integrates passive compliance and multimodal perception.
elements.sent-2411-048: The resulting approach enables adaptive and dexterous manipulation without requiring high precision or prior knowledge, as demonstrated by experiments.
elements.sent-2411-049: The singulating and grasping of thin, deformable objects have long been difficult problems in robotics.
elements.sent-2411-050: Our methodology provides a general framework that addresses this challenge and paves the way for downstream robotic manipulation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-051: Our prior work \cite{flipbot presented a preliminary study restricted to manipulating paper-like objects.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Here, we propose new methods (Section \ref{sec: method ) to expand the manipulation scope to a wide range of thin, deformable objects.}
elements.sent-2411-053: This includes a new problem description (Section \ref{sec:method-pro ), integration of tactile sensors (Section \ref{sec:method-har ), a new slip module (Section \ref{sec:method-slip ) for obtaining sensory information, a double-loop learning structure (Section \ref{sec:method-dual ), and a multisensory encoder (Section \ref{sec:method-enc ) to enhance learning efficacy and performance.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: We conduct extensive experiments (Section \ref{sec:results ) demonstrating the effectiveness of our method on challenging tasks beyond those covered in our previous work, while also elaborating on and evaluating the role of passive compliance (Section \ref{sec:results-nx and Section \ref{sec:results-compliance ) and multisensory integration (Section \ref{sec:results-Visualization ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: A comprehensive component-wise analysis is provided (Section \ref{sec:results-Ablation ), along with a discussion on limitations and future opportunities (Section \ref{sec:results-Discussion ), offering a broader perspective on the applicability and impact of our approach.}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Conventional approaches to deformable object manipulation have revolved around accurately modeling the object's dynamics and subsequently applying model predictive control or trajectory optimization \cite{a1,a4,a5,b1,b4 .}
elements.sent-2411-057: These methods heavily depend on the precise information about the object and any uncertainties encoded in the dynamics model being accurate \cite{a3, b2, c1 .}
elements.sent-2411-058: Some research attempts to model a single thin, deformable object as points or a line based on empirically tuned values, which are often unreliable \cite{l4 .}
elements.sent-2411-059: Overall, these systems are limited to scenarios that can be precisely modeled and lack robustness against the diverse uncertainties encountered in the singulation and grasping of thin, deformable objects.
elements.sent-2411-060: For manipulating thin, deformable objects, model-free reinforcement learning (RL) methods are promising alternatives \cite{d2, e1, e2, k5, f2 to bypass traditional dynamics modeling.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Model-free RL features by learning to control robots directly from raw sensory data through continuous trial and error, introducing a paradigm shift in many robotic areas \cite{h1, h2, h3, i1, i3, j1 .}
elements.sent-2411-062: The manipulation of deformable objects using RL has made significant advances in environments with abundant simulation data \cite{i1, i3 .}
elements.sent-2411-063: However, applying model-free RL to learn singulate and grasp thin, deformable objects remains challenging.
elements.sent-2411-064: These objects' sensitivity to external forces requires the common rigid robot hands to precisely control the contact between fingers and the object, as well as manage the amount and timing of the applied force \cite{l6 .}
elements.sent-2411-065: This creates a high-dimensional control space that is difficult to learn with RL and poses risks of damaging both the robot and the object during real-world exploration.
elements.sent-2411-066: The challenge is exacerbated by the inability of simulators, commonly used to accelerate RL training, to replicate the dynamics of thin, deformable objects accurately \cite{j1 .}
elements.sent-2411-067: Successful learning to manipulate these objects may require simplifying manipulation while ensuring adaptability to varying object properties without relying on high-precision control.
elements.sent-2411-068: Soft robotic hands have recently gained attention for simplifying manipulation while adapting to varying object properties without the need for high-precision control \cite{abondance2020dexterous, flipbot, l6 .}
elements.sent-2411-069: Studies show that soft hands offer passive stability and make manipulations easier to achieve\cite{revision5, revision_R2_3 .}
elements.sent-2411-070: Meanwhile, the inherent compliance of soft robotic hands handles uncertainties during object-fingertip engagement gracefully and limits the potentially damaging forces exerted on thin, deformable objects \cite{l6 .}
elements.sent-2411-071: Complementing the control problem is acquiring the perception needed for singulating and grasping thin, deformable objects.
elements.sent-2411-072: Visual sensors, which most work relies on \cite{depth2, depth3, h3 , provide position and shape information but cannot detect inter-layer interactions or physical properties.}
elements.sent-2411-073: Force/torque sensors, akin to human muscle proprioception \cite{revision3 , offer a comprehensive view of system forces and torques yet are limited to single-point contact data \cite{revision2, flipbot .}}
elements.sent-2411-074: Recent studies have employed tactile sensors \cite{Gelsight, DIGIT to measure local physical properties of deformable objects, such as texture, material, and thickness \cite{l1, l2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-075: However, tactile information alone is limited \cite{m4 , lacking a holistic view of the object’s entire structure and behavior under manipulation.}
elements.sent-2411-076: Therefore, a combination of visual, force/torque, and tactile sensing is required to achieve a comprehensive perception for manipulating thin, deformable objects.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{img/fg5.pdf} \caption{Overview of the presented approach. (A) We train manipulation policies in the real world using RL. During training, the robot proactively interacts with the object to obtain multisensory observations via a slip module. The outer loop selects an action space based on observations, and the inner loop explores within it to output concrete actions for the robot to execute. After training, we deploy the learned policy zero-shot on robots for various tasks. (B) Slip module. The object mask, derived from the segment anything model, is fed into a fully convolutional network to infer pixel-wise affordance maps. The chosen pixel is decoded into slip motion.} \label{fig:5}
elements.sent-2411-077: We learn a neural network policy on a real-world robotic platform to enable the robot to effectively grasp thin, deformable objects using a soft hand.
elements.sent-2411-078: Our method consists of the following stages, as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:5 A.}
elements.sent-2411-079: First, the robot obtains multisensory observations through the slip module (Fig. \ref{fig:5 B).}
elements.sent-2411-080: We use an RGB-D camera to obtain visual observations, allowing for the inference of the slip point and its direction by the slip module.
elements.sent-2411-081: Subsequently, the robot touches and slips with objects and monitors through tactile sensors and force-torque sensors.
elements.sent-2411-082: Thus, the obtained multisensory observations provide insights into unobservable physical information of the environment state.
elements.sent-2411-083: Then, the outer loop selects action space from two granularity levels, coarse and fine, based on the multisensory observations.
elements.sent-2411-084: The coarse action space offers broader movements with sparse discretization, while the fine action space covers a smaller range but benefits from dense discretization.
elements.sent-2411-085: The outer loop utilizes the multisensory encoder to integrate the observations from different senses into a latent vector representation, which then guides the action space selection.
elements.sent-2411-086: Finally, the inner loop explores and learns within the selected action space.
elements.sent-2411-087: Employing the same multisensory encoder, the inner loop maps the observations to the concrete action that is executed by the robot.
elements.sent-2411-088: During training, the rewards obtained by the inner loop are shared with the outer loop, achieving mutual performance improvement.
elements.sent-2411-089: We formulate the problem of singulation and grasping thin, deformable objects as a Markov Decision Process (MDP), with a state space S, action space $A$, a scalar reward function $R$, and a transition probability $P(s_{t+1 |s_t, a_t)$.}
elements.sent-2411-090: At time step $t$, an agent uses a policy $\pi$ to select an action $a_t$ and receives rewards $r_t = R(s_t, a_t)$.
elements.sent-2411-091: Specifically, in our inner-outer loop learning process, the policy function is composed of two components, the outer loop policy $\pi^{out}$ and the inner loop policy $\pi^{in}$.
elements.sent-2411-092: Given the current state, the agent selects a range of action $a^{out}$ using $\pi^{out}$.
elements.sent-2411-093: Then, given $a^{out}$, the agent predicts concrete action $a^{in}$ for the robot to execute using $\pi^{in}$.
elements.sent-2411-094: The objective is to learn an optimal joint policy $(\pi^{out* , \pi^{in* )$ that maximizes the expected discounted reward $r_t$, which can be written as \begin{equation}(\pi^{out*}, \pi^{in*}) = \arg \max_{\substack{\pi^{out} \pi^{in}}} E\left[\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \lambda^t R\left(s_t, \pi^{in}\left(s_t \mid a^{out}_t\right)\right)\right],\end{equation} where ${E [\cdot]$ is the expectation after acting according to the optimal policy in the environment, and $\lambda$ is the discount factor.}}}
elements.sent-2411-095: The reward function $R$ is binary, assigning a value of 1 for task success and 0 otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-096: Fig. \ref{fig:6 A provides an overview of the hardware setup used for policy training.}
elements.sent-2411-097: The setup includes two Universal Robot 5 robot arms, each equipped with an Intel Realsense L515 camera, an ATI gamma Force Torque sensor at the wrist joint, and a two-finger soft robotic hand.
elements.sent-2411-098: The soft robotic hand is based on the design of the fast-PneuNet \cite{revision_R1_4 , a simple pneumatic actuator widely used in the research community.}
elements.sent-2411-099: The whole hand is made of thermoplastic polyurethane (TPU) using a consumer-grade 3D printer.
elements.sent-2411-100: The index and thumb fingertips of each hand are equipped with a vision-based tactile sensor \cite{OurTouch .}
elements.sent-2411-101: Furthermore, the F/T sensor and the soft robotic hand are fixed together, moving and rotating in unison.
elements.sent-2411-102: Section \ref{sec:s4 provides further details about tactile sensors and the soft gripper.}
elements.sent-2411-103: We maneuver the robot using position control within the local/tool coordinate system $H_{\alpha\beta\gamma}$, which is established at the index fingertip, as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:6 C.}
elements.sent-2411-104: The $\alpha$ axis is oriented along the line that connects the two fingertips and lies within the longitudinal plane $\Lambda$ formed by two fingers; the $\gamma$ axis is orthogonal to the $\alpha$ axis and remains within plane $\Lambda$; the $\beta$ axis is perpendicular to the plane formed by $\alpha$ and $\gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-105: Each command issued to the robot is interpreted as movements relative to its current pose.
elements.sent-2411-106: Compared with directly controlling joint angles of the robot arm, this allows us to simplify the dimensions of the action in reinforcement learning and reduce the difficulty of policy learning.
elements.sent-2411-107: The slip module serves two primary functions: obtaining task-related sensory information and providing an initial robot pose to minimize unnecessary exploration.
elements.sent-2411-108: To obtain rich multisensory observations, we employ a slip motion to proactively interact with the object (Fig. \ref{fig:5 B).}
elements.sent-2411-109: This process begins with the robot capturing aligned color and depth images from the top of the object.
elements.sent-2411-110: The color image is processed through the Segment Anything Model (SAM) \cite{SAM to obtain object masks.}
elements.sent-2411-111: These masks are rotated at various angles and fed to the slip network to return a set of affordance maps.
elements.sent-2411-112: Each map contains values corresponding to the slip motion primitive, which are parameterized by the pixel's location and its rotation angle within the map.
elements.sent-2411-113: The pixel with the highest affordance value is selected; its position identifies the slip point, and the corresponding rotation angle of the affordance map determines the slip direction.
elements.sent-2411-114: We next perform the slip motion, where the robot's hand rotates so that the $\alpha$ axis, i.e., the line connecting the two fingers, aligns with the slip direction.
elements.sent-2411-115: The index finger then approaches the object's surface and moves to the predicted slip point.
elements.sent-2411-116: Subsequently, positive air pressure is applied to the soft gripper, enabling the fingers to contact and interact with the object.
elements.sent-2411-117: In this process, we adjust the pose of the end effector to ensure that the plane composed of the $\alpha$ and $\beta$ axes is parallel to the workspace.
elements.sent-2411-118: During the training phase, the workspace's inclination is maintained at 0 degrees.
elements.sent-2411-119: Meanwhile, the position of the slip point in the camera coordinate system is obtained via the camera's depth channel.
elements.sent-2411-120: We beforehand established the relation between the camera and the robot's fingertip coordinate system through hand-eye calibration.
elements.sent-2411-121: After a slip, we record 3D arrays of deformation flows from the tactile sensors as touch observation, along with readings from the F/T sensor as the muscle proprioception, including forces $(f{\alpha , f{\beta , f{\gamma )$ and torques $(m{\alpha , m{\beta , m{\gamma )$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-122: The three main axes of the F/T sensor are aligned with the three corresponding axes of the $H_{\alpha\beta\gamma}$.
elements.sent-2411-123: This alignment makes the force and torque data measured by the sensor more intuitive.
elements.sent-2411-124: Moreover, we crop the area surrounding the slip point on the depth image to serve as visual observation.
elements.sent-2411-125: Through the robot's purposive movements prior to manipulation, observations are made that maximize the information uptake on the relevant properties of the target object.
elements.sent-2411-126: These observations provide insights into the elaborate interplay between static and dynamic sensory cues, such as texture, softness, and friction.
elements.sent-2411-127: The slip network is pre-trained using synthetic data before the robot learns in the real world.
elements.sent-2411-128: Humans annotated slip points on a few images featuring a book, a shirt, and a spring pancake.
elements.sent-2411-129: We employed data augmentation techniques, including rotation, translation, and scaling, on the object masks of these images to simulate different object sizes and positions.
elements.sent-2411-130: More details of the slip network architecture are in Section \ref{sec:s6 .}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{img/fg6.pdf} \caption{Details of components for robust policy learning. (A) Our hardware setup for policy learning. Two robots explore and learn policies simultaneously in parallel environments, updating their policies based on the rewards received. (B) Multisensory encoder architecture. It incorporates a cross-attention mechanism $g_c$ and a transformer encoder $g_\kappa$ that merges features from different senses into a latent vector. FFN denotes the feedforward network. (C and D) Visualization of our action coordinate system, as well as the coarse and fine action spaces.} \label{fig:6}
elements.subsection-2411-004: Manipulation policy training via inner-outer loops
elements.sent-2411-131: During training, within the inner-outer loop policy learning framework, we aim to find an action space selection policy $\pi^{out}$ using the outer loop, and an optimal manipulation policy $\pi^{in}$ under the selected action space using the inner loop.
elements.sent-2411-132: Both loops use the same multisensory observations, and the information about action space selection is included as part of the observations of the inner loop.
elements.sent-2411-133: We utilize Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) \cite{SAC for learning the policies, where the reward obtained by the inner loop is shared with the outer loop during each iteration.}
elements.sent-2411-134: The state for both inner and outer loop policy is defined as $s_t = (o_t^{vis , o_t^{ind , o_t^{thu , o_t^{pro , o_t^{aux )$, where $o_t^{vis}$ refers to the visual observation from the camera, $(o_t^{ind , o_t^{thu )$ refers to the touch observation from the index and thumb fingertips, respectively; $o_t^{prop}$ refers to the muscle proprioception from the F/T sensor, and $o_t^{aux}$ refers to the auxiliary state.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-135: The auxiliary state includes the action space selection and a flag representing whether the current stage belongs to the inner or outer loop.
elements.sent-2411-136: In the outer loop, the flag is assigned a placeholder value of zero to maintain uniformity in the observation format, thereby facilitating the reuse of the multisensory encoder by the inner loop.
elements.sent-2411-137: After the observation is collected, the outer loop selects the action space $a^{out}$ from two different granularities, coarse and fine (Fig. \ref{fig:6 D).}
elements.sent-2411-138: Within the selected action space, the inner loop predicts the action $a^{in}$ that the robot needs to perform.
elements.sent-2411-139: Specifically, the output of the inner loop is rescaled and mapped to different action ranges based on action space selection, and each coordinate of the action $a^{in}$ is discretized accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-140: Formally, the action $a_t^{in}$ is defined as $(x_t,z_t,\theta_t, \Omega)$, as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:6 C.}
elements.sent-2411-141: Here, $(x_t,z_t,\theta_t)$ represents the gripper displacement, representing the relative discrepancy between the current and the desired gripper pose after the slip motion.
elements.sent-2411-142: Specifically, $x_t$ and $z_t$ are the displacement along axis $\alpha$ and $\gamma$, respectively; $\theta_t$ denotes the orientation of the gripper about the axis $\beta$.
elements.sent-2411-143: The parameter $x_t$ adjusts the distance of the index finger from the object's edge.
elements.sent-2411-144: Positioning closer to the edge reduces the required manipulation energy, but being too close or beyond the edge can cause multiple layers to flip up.
elements.sent-2411-145: The parameter $z_t$ regulates the pressure applied on the object's surface to ensure adequate friction during manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-146: The parameter $\theta_t$ enables the robot to distribute varying amounts of force between the two fingers, and the appropriate distribution will facilitate separation and grasping.
elements.sent-2411-147: Additionally, $\Omega$ is a Boolean vector that represents either the closing or opening of the pneumatic finger, which is set to true at the end of the episode to close the gripper.
elements.sent-2411-148: More details of the observation and action are in Section \ref{sec:s5 .}
elements.sent-2411-149: Both inner and outer loop policies are implemented by neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-150: We model the outer loop policy $\pi^{out}$ with the following components: basic encoders, a multisensory encoder, and a multilayer perceptron (MLP), as illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:5 A.}
elements.sent-2411-151: The basic encoders consist of the vision encoder $g_{vis}$, tactile encoders $(g_{ind , g_{thu )$ for each finger, the encoder $g_{pro}$ for F/T measurement, and the auxiliary state encoder $g_{aux}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-152: Given the observation $o_t$, these encoders compress each sensory element into compact, single-dimensional feature vectors $(l_t^{vis , l_t^{ind , l_t^{thu , l_t^{pro , l_t^{aux )$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-153: This process enables the transformation of sensory data into a format that is compatible with the input of the multisensory encoder.
elements.sent-2411-154: The multisensory encoder $g_{mul}$ receives these feature vectors and fuses them into a smaller latent representation $l_t^{out}$: \begin{equation}l_t^{out} = g_{mul}(l_t^{vis}, l_t^{ind}, l_t^{thu}, l_t^{pro}, l_t^{aux}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-155: The MLP then uses the $l_t^{out}$ to output the selection of the action space.
elements.sent-2411-156: For the inner loop policy, we reuse the basic encoders and the multisensory encoder from the outer loop but with a different final MLP block to predict the action.
elements.sent-2411-157: The episode length is fixed to one in order to learn successful policy within a minimal number of steps.
elements.sent-2411-158: At the end of an episode, we provide a positive reward 1 for successfully grasping a single layer of an object.
elements.sent-2411-159: In contrast, results where zero, two or more layers are grasped simultaneously receive a reward of 0.
elements.sent-2411-160: The reward $r_t$ in both the book and fabric environments is determined automatically with information naturally possessed by the manipulated object.
elements.sent-2411-161: Specifically, rewards in the book environment are determined by referencing the page number and in the fabric environment by matching QR code data to a pre-set sequence in the manufacturer's database.
elements.sent-2411-162: To integrate vision, touch, and F/T sensor data, we introduce the multisensory encoder, as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:6 B.}
elements.sent-2411-163: The multisensory encoder $g_{mul}$ consists of a cross-attention block $g_{c}$ \cite{CrossAttention and a transformer encoder $g_{\kappa}$ \cite{Attention .}}
elements.sent-2411-164: The tactile information of the index finger and thumb is complementary as it perceives invisible details about objects from different angles and positions.
elements.sent-2411-165: The cross-attention mechanism is adept at capturing features between such information that is related to each other and has dependencies \cite{CrossAttention .}
elements.sent-2411-166: Within the cross-attention block $g_{c}$, the touch information $l^{ind _t$ and $l^{thu _t$, is fused into a new latent vector $l^{c _t$: \begin{equation}l^{c}_t = g_{c}(l^{ind}_t,l^{thu}_t),\end{equation} Subsequently, $l^{c _t$, along with the other latent vectors of different sensory information, serve as a sequence of inputs to the transformer encoder $g_{\kappa}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-167: It performs mutual fusion encoding among the different sensory data modalities to output a vector $l_t^{\kappa}$ that forms a compact representation for mapping actions: \begin{equation}l_t^{\kappa} = g_{\kappa}(l_t^{vis}, l^{ind}_t,l^{thu}_t, l_t^{c}, l_t^{prop}, l_t^{aux}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-168: The multisensory encoder undergoes an implicit learning process during RL training, avoiding resorting to heuristic-based explicit analysis, which often falters across diverse environments.
elements.sent-2411-169: Network layer details about the multisensory encoder are in Section \ref{sec:s7 .}
elements.sent-2411-170: We train the manipulation policy in the real world (Fig. \ref{fig:6 A).}
elements.sent-2411-171: Operating in parallel environments, robotic arms execute these tasks while continuously updating the policies according to rewards.
elements.sent-2411-172: Specifically, we train our model through trial and error with the following procedure: At the start of each training episode, two robotic arms use their wrist cameras to capture RGB-D images of environments, which are subsequently processed by the slip module to predict the position and direction of the slip motion.
elements.sent-2411-173: Following this prediction, the robot starts to perform a slip motion from its initial posture in each environment to obtain multisensory observations.
elements.sent-2411-174: Then, the robot downloads the latest parameters for both the inner and outer loop policies from the optimizer.
elements.sent-2411-175: The outer loop policy $\pi^{out}$ selects an action space based on multisensory observations.

elements.subsection-2411-010: Robust singulation and grasping across thin deformable objects
elements.sent-2411-188: We tested our system across a variety of scenarios involving the singulation and grasping of thin, deformable objects.
elements.sent-2411-189: These scenarios include turning book pages (Fig. \ref{fig:1 , A to C), emptying paper boxes (Fig. \ref{fig:1 , E to G), displaying fabrics (Fig. \ref{fig:1 , I and J), and picking hotel towels and clothes (Fig. \ref{fig:1 , D and H).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-190: Two hundred trials were conducted for each scenario configuration.
elements.sent-2411-191: The success of each attempt is determined by whether the robot picks only a single layer/object.
elements.sent-2411-192: The robot demonstrated consistent robustness across all scenarios (Fig. \ref{fig:3 D).}
elements.sent-2411-193: In the book page-turning task, high reflectivity and transparency of coated and plastic paper introduced noise in the depth camera, leading to imprecise robot positioning.
elements.sent-2411-194: Nevertheless, the robot's compliance enabled it to adapt to different materials and environmental characteristics, allowing successful singulation and grasping.
elements.sent-2411-195: Experiments with summer suit fabric, hotel towels, and T-shirts, each with significantly different thicknesses from the training objects, further confirmed the robot's robustness.
elements.sent-2411-196: Hybrid materials (Fig. \ref{fig:1 , K to L) also present important challenges.}
elements.sent-2411-197: The first scenario replicates a fabric booklet commonly found in suit shops, where each fabric layer is paired with a corresponding paper detailing its specifications (Fig. \ref{fig:1 K).}
elements.sent-2411-198: The second scenario simulates a culinary environment where spring pancakes are interleaved by baking paper (Fig. \ref{fig:1 L).}
elements.sent-2411-199: Current state-of-the-art methods require additional models \cite{flipbot or explicit sensory data analysis \cite{l3 to manage different material types.}}
elements.sent-2411-200: In contrast, our approach allows the robot to perceive the unique properties of each material through the slip module and adapt policy actions accordingly directly from raw sensory data.
elements.sent-2411-201: This reduces reliance on supplementary models or extensive prior knowledge, allowing the robot to seamlessly handle novel materials.
elements.sent-2411-202: Quantitatively, the learned policy demonstrated impressive generalization to various novel objects, achieving success rates of 97\% for coated paper, 92\% for plastic paper, 98\% for hotel towels, and 86\% for T-shirts.
elements.sent-2411-203: The success rate for mixed materials was commendable but relatively lower.
elements.sent-2411-204: This discrepancy can be attributed to two factors.
elements.sent-2411-205: First, the training process included only homogeneous materials, making generalization to mixed materials more challenging.
elements.sent-2411-206: Second, qualitative observations revealed significant variations in the friction coefficients of mixed materials.
elements.sent-2411-207: The low friction of one layer often led to the simultaneous grasping of multiple layers above it.
elements.sent-2411-208: Additionally, we observed that the layers of summer suit fabrics tended to stick together due to their material characteristics, complicating the isolation of a single layer by the robot.

elements.sent-2411-210: In this section, we explore how our robot singulates and grasps thin, deformable objects under the challenging conditions of different workspace tilt angles (Fig. \ref{fig:2 ).}

elements.sent-2411-217: The learned policy adjusts actions based on multi-sensory inputs, allowing dynamic response to changes in the environment.

elements.subsection-2411-012: Analysis of the underlying mechanisms of the task and the robot
elements.sent-2411-226: To investigate the mechanics of grasping thin deformable objects across different materials, we recorded a successful grasp motion and repeated the action across various areas of the objects.
elements.sent-2411-227: Specifically, using the upper-right corner of the object as the reference point, we incrementally adjust the $\alpha$-axis and $\beta$-axis offsets in action, which cover a 6x6 cm area.
elements.sent-2411-228: This region was divided into 36 individual 1x1 cm cells, with 100 grasp attempts conducted in each cell.
elements.sent-2411-229: Fig. \ref{fig:x presents the performance heatmaps for both paper and fabric materials.}
elements.sent-2411-230: For the paper material, the performance heatmap exhibits a distinct pattern with higher success rates concentrated along the diagonal (angle bisector) of the grid.
elements.sent-2411-231: This aligns with the intuitive understanding that turning a book page diagonally requires the least energy.
elements.sent-2411-232: Meanwhile, areas near the edge of the paper show reduced success rates due to the tendency to grasp multiple layers simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-233: Areas closer to the inner region yield very low success rates (evident in the lower-left corner of the heatmap).
elements.sent-2411-234: This behavior reflects the inherent properties of paper, which has high compressive strength and resistance to bending.
elements.sent-2411-235: When the robot applies force away from the edge, the paper resists deformation, requiring significantly greater force to achieve sufficient bending to turn the page.
elements.sent-2411-236: For fabric material, the performance heatmap followed a slightly different pattern.
elements.sent-2411-237: While higher success rates were also concentrated along the diagonal, the area of optimal performance shifted farther from the edges compared to paper.
elements.sent-2411-238: Furthermore, fabric material demonstrated relatively consistent performance even as the grasp point moved closer to the inner regions.
elements.sent-2411-239: This behavior can be attributed to the lack of compressive strength for the fabric material.
elements.sent-2411-240: Unlike paper, fabric deforms easily and folds or bends in the direction of the applied force.
elements.sent-2411-241: These findings reveal the distinct mechanics underlying the grasping of different materials and suggest the necessity of obtaining comprehensive environmental information through multisensory integration.
elements.sent-2411-242: It also further justifies the design of action parameters.
elements.sent-2411-243: We also conducted experiments to evaluate the role of the soft hand in enabling robust manipulation of thin, deformable objects without requiring precise perception and control.
elements.sent-2411-244: In these experiments, a successful grasp motion was recorded and repeatedly applied to the same layer of the object, while incrementally increasing the object’s height until the grasp failed.
elements.sent-2411-245: This process was carried out on two objects: a printed book and a fabric booklet.
elements.sent-2411-246: Fig. \ref{fig:x B depicts the mean forces along the $\gamma$-axis and their respective ranges during these grasps, as measured by the F/T sensor.}
elements.sent-2411-247: Meanwhile, Fig. \ref{fig:x C illustrates the range of allowable vertical offset before grasp failure occurred.}
elements.sent-2411-248: These results demonstrate that the soft gripper’s compliance effectively maintains the applied force within a relatively narrow range under vertical uncertainty (Fig. \ref{fig:x B).}
elements.sent-2411-249: In contrast, a rigid gripper’s response to slight vertical deviations often results in excessive forces that can trigger the arm’s safety mechanisms or damage the object.
elements.sent-2411-250: For incompressible materials like paper, this challenge is particularly pronounced.
elements.sent-2411-251: The soft gripper accommodates larger vertical offsets without compromising grasp success (Fig. \ref{fig:x C).}
elements.sent-2411-252: This capability is critical for real-world scenarios where factors like camera noise or robot accuracy cause vertical uncertainty.

elements.subsection-2411-013: Evaluating the contribution of soft hand
elements.sent-2411-253: We conducted controlled experiments to quantitatively evaluate the contribution of our soft hand's passive compliance.
elements.sent-2411-254: We trained three position-based policies on different objects: printed books, suit fabric, and a combination of both.
elements.sent-2411-255: These policies mapped pre-measured surface heights directly to actions, eliminating influence from sensors.
elements.sent-2411-256: The initial hand pose was set based on the measured height, replicating the pose after the slip motion.
elements.sent-2411-257: Additionally, we also attempted to use a rigid parallel gripper to singulate and grasp thin, deformable objects with pinch grasps, but neither the position-based policies nor manually set actions were successful.
elements.sent-2411-258: First, we tested the position-based policy trained on the suit fabric (Tab.
elements.sent-2411-259: During testing, each layer of fabric was often compressed due to repeated grasping, resulting in inaccurate initial gripper position and large gaps between the fingers and the object`s surface.
elements.sent-2411-260: Despite lacking sensors to perceive this, the robot achieved a 47\% success rate.
elements.sent-2411-261: In contrast, using a rigid gripper would result in losing contact with the fabric under such conditions.
elements.sent-2411-262: Our soft hand's passive compliance allows it to accommodate position inaccuracies to a certain extent and maintain continuous contact, which is critical for successful manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-263: We then evaluated the performance of the position-based policy trained on printed books (Tab.
elements.sent-2411-264: The policy demonstrated remarkable effectiveness, achieving an 80\% success rate when tested on printed books, a performance close to that of our complete system.
elements.sent-2411-265: The book pages are of uniform thickness, exhibit similar inter-layer interactions, and are difficult to compress.
elements.sent-2411-266: Using a rigid gripper to learn the policy led to collisions and emergency stops during exploration.
elements.sent-2411-267: Similarly, hard-coded actions resulted in collisions and stops due to the robot's repeatability being less than the page thickness.
elements.sent-2411-268: In contrast, the passive compliance of our soft hand absorbs minor imprecisions in operation, ensuring gentle interactions, preventing page tearing, and achieving successful singulation and grasping.
elements.sent-2411-269: In addition, we tested the performance of a position-based policy trained using both printed books and suit fabric (Tab.
elements.sent-2411-270: The distinct properties of these materials led to inconsistent results when identical actions were applied at the same height.
elements.sent-2411-271: As a result, the robot struggled to learn from the reward signal reliably.
elements.sent-2411-272: Nevertheless, we achieved a certain level of success on both training and novel objects, underscoring the value of passive compliance in adapting to diverse material properties without the need for precise control.
elements.sent-2411-273: These results show the critical role of passive compliance in successfully singulating and grasping thin, deformable objects.
elements.sent-2411-274: Our soft hand enabled the robot to perform tasks more successfully and robustly, a feat unachievable with rigid grippers.
elements.sent-2411-275: This demonstrates the principle that passive compliance can bridge the gap between precise control and the unpredictable nature of real-world interactions.

elements.sent-2411-276: In the following analysis, we delve into other components of our proposed approach, including the role of tactile and F/T sensors, the effectiveness of the dual-loop learning approach, and the impact of using the attention-based encoder.
elements.sent-2411-277: To evaluate the influence of tactile and F/T sensors, we compared our complete approach, ``Ours," with the ablation methods: ``OnlyVision" (OV), which excludes both tactile and F/T sensors during policy learning, and ``NoTouch" (NT), which excludes only touch information during policy learning.
elements.sent-2411-278: The OV method, relying solely on depth observation, exhibits a feature bias that manifests as a heavy preference for height information.
elements.sent-2411-279: The learned policy fails to internalize the principles and mechanisms for singulating and grasping thin, deformable objects, affecting the overall performance and generalization.
elements.sent-2411-280: This limitation was reflected in the low success rate of the OV method across various objects (Fig. \ref{fig:3 D).}
elements.sent-2411-281: In contrast, both NT and Ours incorporated multimodal sensors, providing a more comprehensive understanding of the environment.
elements.sent-2411-282: This allowed the learned policy to focus on critical mechanisms for task success.
elements.sent-2411-283: Our method, which integrates additional tactile sensors compared to NT, is able to obtain local contact features, further enhancing the robustness and generalization ability of learned policy, as evidenced by its superior performance (Fig. \ref{fig:3 D).}
elements.sent-2411-284: These findings highlight the importance of multi-modal perception in delivering comprehensive task information, thereby preventing feature dependence and facilitating effective policy learning for singulating and grasping thin, deformable objects.
elements.sent-2411-285: Next, we evaluated our dual-loop learning structure by comparing it with a ``SingleLoop” (SL) variant, which directly explores the entire action space to learn policies.
elements.sent-2411-286: Experiments show that our method significantly outperforms the SL method within the same timeframe (Fig. \ref{fig:3 H).}
elements.sent-2411-287: Notably, we found that the choice of action space is highly correlated with the material properties.
elements.sent-2411-288: For instance, with fabric materials used in training, a fine action space was predominantly selected.
elements.sent-2411-289: In contrast, for paper materials, the action space was more evenly distributed between coarse and fine.
elements.sent-2411-291: This can be attributed to the differences in material surface properties and the noise characteristics of the depth camera.
elements.sent-2411-292: Fabric materials, due to their rough surfaces, generate sufficient friction with small movements from the initial pose after the slip motion.
elements.sent-2411-293: Conversely, the smooth surface of book pages necessitates larger movements to achieve the same effect.
elements.sent-2411-294: Additionally, the depth camera exhibits smaller noise on the fabric and larger noise on the paper, prompting the robot to choose a larger action space for the paper to compensate for inaccuracies in the initial positioning.
elements.sent-2411-295: The dual-loop learning structure effectively reduces the range of actions that need to be explored by first selecting the appropriate action space.
elements.sent-2411-296: This approach enhanced real-world learning efficiency by avoiding the extensive and often ineffective exploration of the entire action space as in the SL method.
elements.sent-2411-297: We also compared the impact of using an attention mechanism to fuse sensory inputs compared to a method without it, termed ``NoAttention (NA)." The attention mechanism, known for its ability to consider the interdependencies between different sensory inputs, creates more meaningful and context-aware feature representations.
elements.sent-2411-298: Experiment results consistently showed that our method with an attention-based multisensory encoder outperformed the NA method in all evaluation scenarios, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed encoder.
elements.subsection-2411-015: Visualization the role of multisensory information
elements.sent-2411-299: To examine how our system integrates different sensory streams and their respective roles, we conducted a series of controlled experiments and visualizations.
elements.sent-2411-300: We collected 40 sets of sensory inputs from different pages of a book and a fabric booklet for feature clustering.
elements.sent-2411-301: Each set included observations from tactile and F/T sensor measurements, along with fused latent features generated by the multisensory encoder.
elements.sent-2411-302: In the t-SNE visualization for F/T sensing (Fig. \ref{fig:4 A), the close and overlapping embeddings suggest that different objects share similar properties in the F/T feature space.}
elements.sent-2411-303: Fig. \ref{fig:4 (D to E) reveal a clear correlation between F/T sensing and the number of layers in the book and fabric booklet, indicating that F/T sensing primarily reflects physical interaction properties such as pressure, weight, or finger positioning, rather than the material properties.}
elements.sent-2411-304: In contrast, t-SNE visualization of touch inputs (Fig. \ref{fig:4 B) shows two relatively independent clusters, indicating that tactile sensations are more closely related to material attributes like texture and firmness.}
elements.sent-2411-305: Fig. \ref{fig:4 (F to G) displays the touch data visualizations of the book and fabric booklet.}
elements.sent-2411-306: The t-SNE visualization of the fused feature vector (Fig. \ref{fig:4 C) displays two independent groups with clear separation, demonstrating that integrating multisensory inputs provides a richer, more comprehensive synthesis of information than any single sensory modality.}
elements.sent-2411-307: The passive compliance of the soft hand enables robots to dexterously and reliably handle various thin, deformable materials, being robust to uncertainties while minimizing the risk of damage and reducing the need for precise perception and control.
elements.sent-2411-308: Tactile and F/T sensors equipped on the soft hand collect extensive context information about manipulation through the sliding of a finger across surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-309: The integration of multi-sensor data allows the learning process to concentrate on the core principles of the task rather than appearing feature dependence.
elements.sent-2411-310: To avoid complex modeling, we employ model-free RL to learn manipulation policies that drive the soft hand directly from raw sensory inputs.
elements.sent-2411-311: Learning efficiency is a critical consideration in this context.
elements.sent-2411-312: An initial pose is provided after slip motion, eliminating the need to learn how to approach the object.
elements.sent-2411-313: The proposed dual-loop learning structure reduces unnecessary exploration, and the attention-based multi-sensory encoder enhances feature fusion, both of which improve learning efficiency and performance.
elements.sent-2411-314: Additionally, rewards are autonomously determined by reading page numbers or QR code data.
elements.sent-2411-315: Automated recycling mechanisms are integrated to reset the environment, thereby minimizing the need for human intervention.
elements.sent-2411-316: The result is a robot of high robustness and generalization ability.
elements.sent-2411-317: The system was validated on a variety of challenging tasks that were beyond the reach of prior studies.
elements.sent-2411-318: Our controlled experiments contained 36 test scenes, blending various task scenarios, object types, and workspace tilt angles.
elements.sent-2411-319: We also demonstrated the broad potential of our method, from helping a violinist turn a music sheet to making spring pancake wraps (Movie 1).
elements.sent-2411-320: Additionally, Section \ref{sec:s2 and Movie S1 provide our further exploration regarding bag opening and cloth manipulation.}
elements.sent-2411-321: We see several limitations and opportunities for future work.
elements.sent-2411-322: First, our learned policy relies on multisensory information obtained before manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-323: This is a significant advantage, enabling one-shot manipulation and enhanced efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-324: However, the reliance on pre-obtained multisensory information has its inherent limitations.
elements.sent-2411-325: A major opportunity for future research would use the multisensory information gathered after manipulation (e.g. after the two fingers are closed).
elements.sent-2411-326: This could allow for active determination of whether multiple layers have been grasped and guide subsequent actions of the robot.
elements.sent-2411-327: Another limitation is that when one layer has significantly lower friction than the other in stacked objects, one-handed manipulation is not effective enough for singulation.
elements.sent-2411-328: Even humans will encounter difficulties in such conditions.
elements.sent-2411-329: Just as humans would use both hands to coordinate manipulation, one opportunity for future work is to learn a dual-hand system to address this situation.
elements.sent-2411-330: Furthermore, our current manipulation policy obtains visual perception from the depth image without fully utilizing the color image.
elements.sent-2411-331: The learned policy is independent of information such as object color, which eliminates the need to retrain or fine-tune across different environments.
elements.sent-2411-332: However, depth information ignores details present in color images that may offer insights into materials and textures.
elements.sent-2411-333: Integrating the RGB image into the network potentially improves policy performance.
elements.sent-2411-334: Moreover, our slip module is simple and effective in predicting slip points and is learned prior to RL policy training.
elements.sent-2411-335: However, the slip module may require additional training data when faced with novel objects of different structures or shapes.
elements.sent-2411-336: This limitation stems from its inherent lack of world knowledge and inability to engage in physical reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-337: For instance, it cannot understand why a slip point would be more effective on the upper-right corner of a book than on the spine.
elements.sent-2411-338: Such knowledge usually reflects humans' high-level cognitive intelligence and is distinct from the focus of this paper, which is on low-level sensory-motor intelligence.
elements.sent-2411-339: Looking ahead, an interesting direction for future research is to leverage large foundation models \cite{llm1, llm4 to learn high-level planning and reasoning about where to perform the slip motion.}
elements.sent-2411-340: Such models have built extensive world knowledge and representations, enabling direct generalization to a wider range of objects and facilitating robot deployment in more complex environments.
elements.sent-2411-341: Additionally, the in-hand manipulation model can be learned to align the object to the appropriate position after grasping, which could be widely used in the subsequent folding of clothes or origami tasks.
elements.sent-2411-342: This study presents the method for learning to singulate and grasp thin, deformable objects.
elements.sent-2411-343: The learned policy drives our robot equipped with soft pneumatic grippers, achieving a significant leap over past work in terms of graspable object variety and performance.
elements.sent-2411-344: Notably, our training uses only printer paper and winter fabric on flat surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-345: However, the robot successfully handles various deformable objects with different physical properties (coated paper, hotel towels, T-shirts), hybrid materials (fabrics separated by introduction pages or spring pancakes separated by baking paper), and gravitational effects.
elements.sent-2411-346: Our results suggest that the extraordinary complexity of manipulating thin, deformable objects can be tamed with embodied multisensory integration and compliance in the soft gripper.
elements.sent-2411-347: By enabling the robot to adapt to the complex dynamics of deformable objects without relying on precise control, our approach opens new possibilities for robots to operate in unstructured environments and perform tasks that require delicate handling.
elements.sent-2411-348: Future work may involve extending this framework to more complex manipulation tasks and further improving the efficiency and generalization of the learning process.

elements.section-2411-008: Additional experiments on garments and bag opening
elements.sent-2411-353: To further validate the robustness of our learned policy and the flexibility of our method, we conducted two supplemental experiments.
elements.sent-2411-354: Both experiments employed the previously learned RL policy without fine-tuning, merely adding extra data to retrain the slip module.
elements.sent-2411-355: Our first experiment investigated the task of bag opening.
elements.sent-2411-356: To adapt the slip module to this task, we additionally annotated data regarding slip points and directions on plastic bags (Fig. \ref{fig:s1 A).}

elements.sent-2411-365: Our experiments encountered several cases where the robot failed to achieve the desired singulation and grasping.
elements.sent-2411-366: The most frequent failure was the unintentional grasping of multiple layers.
elements.sent-2411-367: We observed two primary causes for this form of failure.
elements.sent-2411-368: The first cause relates to a low friction coefficient between certain object layers.
elements.sent-2411-369: As demonstrated in Fig. \ref{fig:s2 A, when Layer B or C has an exceptionally smooth surface, the frictional forces between them are significantly less than those between layers A and B.}
elements.sent-2411-370: As a result, layers A and B are simultaneously grasped.
elements.sent-2411-371: This phenomenon is primarily observed in scenarios involving fabrics and mixed materials, which is due to the variations in surface smoothness among fabrics and the natural disparity in friction coefficients among different materials.
elements.sent-2411-372: The second cause of failure, as illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:s2 B, arises when layers are stuck together.}
elements.sent-2411-373: This is commonly observed in contexts involving paper where even manual separation requires considerable effort.
elements.sent-2411-374: The adhesion often results from microscopic structural factors or external variables like ink, printing, and Van der Waals forces.
elements.sent-2411-375: Under such conditions, the frictional force between layers A and B is significantly greater than between layers B and C, leading to multiple layers being grasped together.
elements.sent-2411-376: As discussed in the experiments of book page-turning with printer paper, we attempted 200 flips and succeeded 188 times, yielding a success rate of 94\%.
elements.sent-2411-377: Among the 12 unsuccessful attempts, 11 were attributed to tight adhesion between pages and one to noise errors in the depth camera reading.
elements.sent-2411-378: In addition, we observed objects slipping out of the fingers during manipulation due to camera noise or inappropriate action prediction, especially in scenes not encountered during training.
elements.sent-2411-379: Moreover, when the workspace has a high tilt angle, it is easier to grasp multiple layers due to the altered friction dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-380: Movie S2 shows the representative failure cases during experiments.
elements.sent-2411-381: We used a vision-based tactile sensor of fingertip dimensions based on \cite{OurTouch .}
elements.sent-2411-382: While sensors like GelSight \cite{Gelsight and DIGIT \cite{DIGIT can offer high-resolution tactile feedback, they are constrained by their larger dimensions and flat geometry; hence they are hard to integrate into a space the size of a human fingertip.}}
elements.sent-2411-383: In contrast, our sensor is able to provide a large field of view in a compact form factor, benefiting from the compound-eye imaging system \cite{OurTouch , making it ideal for integration into the minuscule fingertips of soft grippers.}
elements.sent-2411-384: Our tactile sensor consists of three crucial components, as illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:s2 C: the touch, imaging, and support modules.}
elements.sent-2411-385: The touch module features a curved elastomer layer designed to conform to the fingertip of our soft gripper, and an acrylic plate.
elements.sent-2411-386: The elastomer layer is constructed by a transparent layer embedded with a random color pattern, a white protective layer, and a black layer designed to stabilize light intensity variations.
elements.sent-2411-387: The imaging sub-system beneath the curved touching layers includes a microlens array, a pinhole structure to define the aperture, and a Complementary Metal Oxide Semiconductor (CMOS) image sensor.
elements.sent-2411-388: Enclosing these is the support module, a 3D-printed shell that provides the structural integrity required for the effective functioning of the tactile sensor.

elements.section-2411-015: Experiments of training policies on different gravitational conditions
elements.sent-2411-430: In this section, we explore the policy performance trained under conditions that include different gravity effects.
elements.sent-2411-431: We compared three cases: our original policy trained only on the horizontal plane (denoted as OnlyZ) and a policy trained at workspace tilt angles including 0 and 30 degrees (denoted as Zand30) and 0 and 60 degrees (denoted as Zand60).
elements.sent-2411-432: The training objects for all cases are the same, including printer books and winter suit fabrics.
elements.sent-2411-433: Unseen objects for testing are those cases in Fig. 3D, excluding the training objects.
elements.sent-2411-434: During training, the two robotic arms are each responsible for a tilt angle.
elements.sent-2411-435: After every five attempts, the researcher exchanges objects.
elements.sent-2411-436: Fig. \ref{fig:s5 A and 5B show the difference in performance when training includes different gravity effects, compared to OnlyZ.}
elements.sent-2411-437: The results show that Zand60 significantly improves the average performance in the test under the 30 and 60-degree conditions (Fig. S5B).
elements.sent-2411-438: This proves that including different gravity effects in the training can enhance the overall performance.
elements.sent-2411-439: Meanwhile, Zand30 also achieved performance improvement under corresponding conditions, although the improvement was smaller (Fig. S5A).
elements.sent-2411-440: This reflects that objects on the physical characteristics of a workspace with a 30-degree tilt angle are closer to those of a 0-degree workspace.
elements.sent-2411-441: Fig. \ref{fig:s5 C shows the training curves of these three cases.}
elements.sent-2411-442: Including different workspace tilt angles increases the diversity of situations encountered during the training phase.
elements.sent-2411-443: This variation demands more exploration from the RL agent, thus extending the training time to reach convergence.
elements.sent-2411-444: These results also demonstrate the potential that, when hardware conditions permit, our approach can be extended to more parallel environments.
elements.sent-2411-445: This can not only significantly accelerate the learning process but also cover a wider range of training objects and gravitational effects, further improving the robot's performance.

elements.sent-2411-448: Despite notable progress in robotic manipulation \cite{fp0,fp6, zhu2022challenges and the deployment of numerous commercial robotic platforms, such as Covariant’s AI-driven picking solutions \cite{fp7 and Boston Dynamics' Stretch warehouse robot \cite{fp9 , even the most advanced robots fall short of human performance when manipulating thin, deformable objects.}}}
elements.sent-2411-449: We suggest that advancements in thin deformable object manipulation hinge on achieving “imprecise dexterity” — the ability to perform dexterous manipulation through robust and adaptive behaviors despite imprecision in control, movement, and perception.
elements.sent-2411-450: Singulation and grasping thin, deformable objects are common daily tasks, such as turning book pages (Fig. \ref{fig:1 ).}
elements.sent-2411-451: Visual sensors often fail to capture essential physical properties like friction and stiffness, and can be occluded by fingers or objects, necessitating heavy reliance on touch and proprioception for feedback on object interaction.
elements.figure-2411-020: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{img/fg1.pdf} \caption{Our robot assists a violinist with page-turning during performances.} \label{fig:1}
elements.sent-2411-452: Here, we present a robust approach for singulating and grasping a variety of thin deformable objects in real-world scenarios that are beyond the capability of prior published work in robotics (Movie 1\footnote{All the videos are available at https://robotll.github.io/LTDOM/ ).}
elements.sent-2411-453: One of the key components is our soft, underactuated two-finger hand.
elements.sent-2411-454: Different thin deformable objects exhibit various behaviors under external forces; for instance, fabric dents easily while pages resist pressure but yield to shear forces.
elements.sent-2411-455: Compared with the gripper in Flipbot \cite{flipbot , we maintained its general design but augmented its sensory capabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-456: Our hand integrates tactile sensors at each fingertip alongside the original F/T sensors, offering passive compliance, touch, and muscle proprioception similar to a human hand.
elements.sent-2411-457: The addition of tactile sensing enables the robot to perceive local contact features closely linked to material properties such as texture and hardness.
elements.sent-2411-458: In contrast, Flipbot’s reliance solely on global force information provided by F/T sensors limits its performance and generalization potential, restricting it to the paper-like object.
elements.sent-2411-459: Like Flipbot \cite{flipbot , we learned a direct mapping from perception to robot control using model-free RL in the real world to drive our soft hand.}
elements.sent-2411-460: Model-based methods, while powerful in structured domains, struggle in deformable object manipulation due to the inherent uncertainty and complexity of thin deformable objects.
elements.sent-2411-461: Their high-dimensional configuration spaces, coupled with partially observable dynamics and large variations in material properties (e.g., bending stiffness, friction), pose significant challenges for analytical modeling.
elements.sent-2411-462: In contrast, model-free RL learn manipulation policies end-to-end from trial-and-error exploration, without requiring explicit modeling of object dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-463: However, model-free RL alone was insufficient for learning the robust policy as presented in our video.
elements.sent-2411-464: We found that the reward sparsity in these tasks makes robot impossible to discover effective policies from scratch, and acquiring task-relevant perception remains a problem.
elements.sent-2411-465: In Flipbot \cite{flipbot , the slip motion is hardcoded and fixed for specific scenarios, making it impossible to generalize to different object sizes and positions.}
elements.sent-2411-466: Now, slip motion is guided by a neural network that receives inputs from a wrist-mounted camera and is trained on data reflecting human intuition (e.g., turning book pages from the upper right).
elements.sent-2411-467: Such position-specific slips not only provide the robot with a proper initial pose, reducing the search space for policy learning, but also generate rich, dynamic interactions that expose latent physical properties like flexural rigidity and friction.
elements.sent-2411-468: Another component is a hierarchical, dual-loop learning structure, designed to improve learning efficiency—an essential factor for real-world reinforcement learning.
elements.sent-2411-469: Flipbot \cite{flipbot introduced a reset mechanism and an automatic reward signal based on the page number, significantly reducing human intervention and enabling policy learning within four hours.}
elements.sent-2411-470: Building on this framework, we extend the training setup to include fabric materials.
elements.sent-2411-471: This increased variety necessitates greater exploration and longer training durations.
elements.sent-2411-472: To address this, the dual-loop structure accelerates learning through the structured and efficient exploration and exploitation of the action space.
elements.sent-2411-473: The presented study achieves a significant leap over the existing work in terms of graspable object variety and performance, marking a meaningful step toward human-level sensorimotor intelligence.
elements.sent-2411-474: Notably, our training only uses printer paper and winter fabric on flat surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-475: Yet when deployed in environments, the robot successfully handles various deformable objects with different physical properties (coated paper, hotel towel, summer suit fabric), hybrid materials (fabrics separated by the introduction page or spring pancakes separated by baking paper), and gravitational effects.
elements.sent-2411-476: Our results suggest that the extraordinary complexity of manipulating thin, deformable objects can be tamed with the integration of embodied multi-sensory integration and passive compliance.
elements.sent-2411-477: The main contributions of this article are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-478: We propose a novel approach for singulation and grasping thin, deformable objects by controlling a soft, underactuated hand that integrates passive compliance and multi-modal perception.
elements.sent-2411-479: To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to successfully demonstrate the singulation and grasping across such a wide range of thin deformable objects under challenging real-world conditions.
elements.sent-2411-480: We introduce a slip module that actively collects rich multisensory information and provides a favorable initial pose prior to manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-481: This enhances task awareness and learning performance.
elements.sent-2411-482: We present a dual-loop learning structure that adaptively selects action space granularity based on sensory input.
elements.sent-2411-483: The mechanism improves sample efficiency and accelerates policy convergence.
elements.sent-2411-484: We validate our approach through extensive experiments, including ablation studies, quantitative evaluations of passive compliance, and analysis of the underlying mechanisms enabling successful manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-485: This journal paper evolves upon the conference paper presented in \cite{flipbot .}
elements.sent-2411-486: Section \ref{sec: method introduces the problem formulation (Section \ref{sec:method-pro ), a slip module (Section \ref{sec:method-slip ) for obtaining sensory information, and a double-loop learning structure.}}}
elements.sent-2411-487: We conduct extensive experiments (Section \ref{sec:results ) demonstrating the effectiveness of our method, while also evaluating the role of passive compliance (Section \ref{sec:results-nx and Section \ref{sec:results-compliance ) and multisensory integration (Section \ref{sec:results-Visualization ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-488: A comprehensive component-wise analysis is provided (Section \ref{sec:results-Ablation ), along with a discussion on limitations and future opportunities (Section \ref{sec:results-Discussion ).}}
elements.sent-2411-489: Deformable object manipulation encompasses a range of strategies.
elements.sent-2411-490: One line of research involves model-based methods, which often revolve around accurately modeling the object's dynamics and subsequently applying model predictive control or trajectory optimization \cite{a1,a4,a5 .}
elements.sent-2411-491: These methods heavily depend on the precise information about the object and any uncertainties encoded in the dynamics model being accurate \cite{a3, c1, l4 .}
elements.sent-2411-492: In the context of thin, deformable objects, model-based approaches face significant challenges due to multiple sources of uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-493: Accurately estimating physical properties such as bending stiffness and surface friction is inherently difficult, as these parameters vary even among seemingly identical objects due to differences in texture, wear, and manufacturing variability.
elements.sent-2411-494: Moreover, such objects exhibit high-dimensional and nonlinear configuration spaces, where small action perturbations result in drastically different deformations, rendering reliable forward prediction intractable.
elements.sent-2411-495: These uncertainties are further exacerbated by sensor occlusion, depth perception errors, and complex finger-object interactions, which are difficult to incorporate into analytical models.
elements.sent-2411-496: Another line of research that has gained substantial attention for deformable object manipulation is model-free approaches.
elements.sent-2411-497: Notably, Navarro-Alarcon et al. \cite{navarro2013model proposed a model-free visual servoing framework to control the deformation of elastic objects using closed-loop feedback, bypassing the need for explicit object modeling.}
elements.sent-2411-498: Concurrently, learning-based methods have prominence in deformable object manipulation \cite{d1, d2 , which is largely attributed to the capability to infer action from sensory input, bypassing the complexities of direct physical modeling \cite{e1, e2, k5, f2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-499: Within this domain, model-free reinforcement learning (RL) methods \cite{ibarz2021train, ccalicsir2019model have shown great potential for manipulating thin, deformable objects.}
elements.sent-2411-500: Model-free RL features by learning to control robots directly from raw sensory data through continuous trial and error, introducing a paradigm shift in many robotic areas, including whole-body control for door opening \cite{h1 , planetary exploration using legged robots \cite{h2 , and autonomous flight of micro aerial swarms \cite{h3 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-501: Moreover, sim-to-real transfer techniques have further extended these capabilities to real-world applications \cite{j1 .}
elements.sent-2411-502: However, applying model-free RL to learn thin, deformable object manipulation remains challenging.
elements.sent-2411-503: The challenge is exacerbated by the inability of simulators, commonly used to accelerate RL training, to replicate the dynamics of thin, deformable objects accurately.
elements.sent-2411-504: Blanco-Mulero et al. \cite{blanco2024benchmarking systematically analyzed this gap, revealing that state-of-the-art simulators struggle to accurately replicate the nuanced physical behaviors of cloth.}
elements.sent-2411-505: Soft robotic hands \cite{soft1, soft2, soft3 have recently gained attention for simplifying manipulation while adapting to varying object properties without the need for high-precision control \cite{abondance2020dexterous, l6 .}}
elements.sent-2411-506: Studies show that soft hands offer passive stability and make manipulations easier to achieve \cite{revision5, revision_R2_3 .}
elements.figure-2411-021: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{img/fg2.pdf} \caption{Overview of the presented approach. (A) We train manipulation policies in the real world using RL. During training, the robot proactively interacts with the object to obtain multisensory observations via a slip module. The outer loop selects an action space based on observations, and the inner loop explores within it to output concrete actions for the robot to execute. After training, we deploy the learned policy zero-shot on robots for various tasks. (B) Slip module. The object mask, derived from the segment anything model, is fed into a fully convolutional network to infer pixel-wise affordance maps. The chosen pixel is decoded into slip motion, with its 3D coordinates derived from the depth image.} \label{fig:2}
elements.sent-2411-507: Our method consists of the following stages, as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:2 A.}
elements.sent-2411-508: First, the robot obtains multisensory observations through the slip module (Fig. \ref{fig:2 B).}
elements.sent-2411-509: The slip module is pre-trained using data reflecting human preferences.
elements.sent-2411-510: Then, the outer loop selects the action space from two granularity levels, coarse and fine, based on the multisensory observations.
elements.sent-2411-511: Employing the same multisensory encoder, the inner loop maps the observations to the robot's concrete action.
elements.sent-2411-512: Fig. \ref{fig:3 A provides an overview of the hardware setup used for policy learning.}
elements.sent-2411-513: The setup includes two Universal Robot 5 robot arms, each equipped with an Intel RealSense L515 camera, an ATI gamma Force Torque sensor at the wrist joint, and a two-finger soft robotic hand.
elements.sent-2411-514: Section \ref{sec:s4 provides further details about tactile sensors, and we discuss our gripper design in Section \ref{sec:s9 .}}
elements.sent-2411-515: We maneuver the robot using position control within the local/tool coordinate system $H_{\alpha\beta\gamma}$, which is established at the index fingertip, as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:3 C.}
elements.sent-2411-516: Compared with directly controlling the joint angles of the robot arm, this allows us to simplify the dimensions of the action and reduce the difficulty of policy learning.
elements.figure-2411-022: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{img/fg3.pdf} \caption{Details of components for robust policy learning. (A) Our hardware setup for policy learning. Two robots explore and learn policies simultaneously in parallel environments, updating their policies based on the rewards received. (B) Multisensory encoder architecture. It incorporates a cross-attention mechanism $g_c$ and a transformer encoder $g_\kappa$ that merges features from different senses into a latent vector. FFN denotes the feedforward network.
